Our prayers must be accompanied by serious effort; otherwise, we mock God. We are not limited to certain times when we address the King of kings, but have freedom to go to him at all times; approaches to the throne of grace are never inappropriate. But a sense of God’s displeasure and the afflictions of his people cause sorrow to the children of God, under which no earthly delights can bring comfort. The king encouraged Nehemiah to share his thoughts. This gave him boldness to speak; much more may the invitation Christ has given us to pray, and the promise that we will succeed, encourage us to come boldly to the throne of grace. Nehemiah prayed to the God of heaven, as infinitely above even this mighty monarch. He lifted up his heart to that God who understands the language of the heart. Nor should we ever engage in any pursuit in which it would be wrong for us to seek and expect Divine direction, assistance, and blessing. There was an immediate answer to his prayer; for the people of Jacob never sought the God of Jacob in vain.

When Nehemiah had considered the matter, he told the Jews that God had put it into his heart to build the wall of Jerusalem. He does not undertake to do it without them. By encouraging ourselves and one another to do what is good, we strengthen ourselves and one another for it. We are weak in our duty when we are cold and careless.

The hatred of the serpent’s seed against the cause of Christ is not limited to any age or nation. The application to ourselves is clear. The church of God asks for our help. Is it not desolate, and exposed to assaults? Does the consideration of its low condition cause you grief? Let not business, pleasure, or supporting a group so engage your attention that Zion and her welfare are nothing to you.
